methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $87M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Fairmat
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $350M

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Osaro is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company founded by Derik Pridmore and Michael Kahane that has raised $86.8M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private robotics firms at the same stage—including 1X Technologies, Bedrock Robotics, Dexterity, Collaborative Robotics, and Fairmat—plus one public comparable.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the $87M total raised as a proxy base and applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, yielding $165M. Peer-median methodology examines six same-sector, same-stage comparables with a median valuation of $1.1B and a range spanning $300M to $10.0B, directly implying $1.1B for Osaro.

The triangulated analysis across both methods supports a valuation range of $140M (low) to $1.2B (high), with a median of $620M. The wide dispersion reflects material heterogeneity within the peer set—specifically the outsized valuations of 1X Technologies at $10.0B versus tighter clustering of others in the $350M–$1.8B band. Key uncertainties include unit economics relative to peers, path to commercial scale in manufacturing automation, and whether Osaro's technology differentiation justifies positioning toward the peer distribution's upper or median quartile. Capital efficiency and customer concentration warrant close examination.
